title: Negotiation Foundations: Distributive vs Integrative
simple_html:
Every negotiation falls somewhere between two basic types. Knowing which one you are in — and not treating every deal like a fight — is the single most important foundation skill.

The two types

  • Distributive negotiation is "claiming value" — dividing a fixed pie. One side's gain is the other's loss (win-lose). Haggling over the price of a used car is the classic case: every dollar you save is a dollar the seller loses.
  • Integrative negotiation is "creating value" — growing the pie before dividing it (win-win). By trading across multiple issues that each side values differently, both can end up better off.

Why the difference matters

Most real business deals have integrative potential, but people instinctively treat them as distributive fights. This fixed-pie mindset causes negotiators to leave value on the table — they argue over one issue (price) and ignore others (delivery time, volume, payment terms, warranty) where a smart trade could help both sides.

How to create value

  • Find multiple issues, not just one.
  • Look for differences in priorities — give the other side what is cheap for you but valuable to them, and vice versa.
  • Share some information about your interests to find these trades.

Key takeaways

  • Distributive = dividing a fixed pie (win-lose); integrative = growing it (win-win).
  • Beware the fixed-pie myth — most deals have hidden win-win potential.
  • Multiple issues + different priorities = room for value-creating trades.
  • Almost every real negotiation has both elements: you create value, then claim it.
intermediate_html:
This lesson is the conceptual hinge of the negotiation half of the course. The distributive-integrative distinction frames how you prepare, what tactics you use, and how much value you and your counterpart ultimately capture. Mastering it prevents the most common and expensive negotiation error: treating a value-creating opportunity as a value-claiming brawl.

The two paradigms

DistributiveIntegrative
PieFixedExpandable
GoalClaim the largest shareCreate then claim value
RelationshipOften short-termOften ongoing
InformationGuardedShared (selectively)
IssuesSingle (e.g., price)Multiple, tradeable
OutcomeWin-loseWin-win (Pareto-improving)

Distributive: claiming value

In a pure distributive deal, the core variables are each party's reservation price (walk-away point) and the resulting ZOPA (zone of possible agreement) — covered fully in Lesson 7. The key tactics are anchoring (making the first offer to set the reference point), managing concessions, and not revealing your reservation price. The first mover's anchor strongly shapes the final figure.

Integrative: creating value

Value creation rests on a few engines: (1) multiple issues so trades are possible; (2) differences in priorities — logrolling, where each party concedes on issues it cares less about in exchange for issues it cares more about; (3) differences in forecasts, risk tolerance, or time, which enable contingent contracts; and (4) adding issues or resources to expand scope. The conceptual goal is to reach the Pareto frontier — the set of agreements where no party can be made better off without making the other worse off.

Worked example: logrolling

A supplier and retailer negotiate. The retailer cares most about price (cash-strapped) and less about delivery speed. The supplier cares most about delivery flexibility (it smooths their production) and can afford a modest price cut. A distributive negotiator fights only over price and stalls. An integrative one trades: the supplier grants a price reduction (cheap relative to its margin) in exchange for flexible delivery windows (valuable to its operations). Both improve versus a price-only fight — a Pareto improvement created by exploiting the difference in priorities.

The negotiator's dilemma

The two modes are in tension. Creating value requires sharing information; claiming value rewards concealing it. Share too much and you may be exploited; share too little and you never find the trades. This negotiator's dilemma (Lax & Sebenius, 1986) is managed by reciprocal, incremental information exchange — reveal interests (not your reservation price), test for shared gains, and protect your claiming position.

How to apply it

  • Diagnose the structure first: single-issue, one-off → mostly distributive; multi-issue, ongoing → integrative potential.
  • Unbundle issues and rank your priorities and (estimated) theirs to find logrolling trades.
  • Ask interest questions (Lesson 2) before bargaining over numbers.
  • Create first, then claim: expand the pie, then negotiate the split.
  • Guard your reservation price even while sharing interests.

This lesson sets up principled negotiation (Lesson 6 — a method for value creation), the analytics of BATNA/ZOPA (Lesson 7 — the engine of value claiming), and tactics, biases, and ethics (Lesson 8).
full_html:
The distributive-integrative distinction is the foundational typology of negotiation theory and the lens through which the rest of the syllabus is best understood. It determines preparation, tactic selection, information strategy, and ultimately how much joint and individual value is realized. The central practical claim of modern negotiation research is that most consequential negotiations contain integrative potential that fixed-pie thinking systematically destroys.

Two structures of interdependence

Distributive (zero-sum) bargaining concerns the division of a fixed resource: gains to one party are losses to the other. The canonical analysis is a single continuous issue (price) bounded by each side's reservation point. Integrative (variable-sum) bargaining concerns the joint creation of value before its division: because parties value issues differently, trades can make both better off. Walton & McKersie's (1965) foundational work in labor relations first formalized this duality, distinguishing distributive bargaining from integrative bargaining (and adding attitudinal structuring and intra-organizational bargaining). The terms "creating value" and "claiming value" come from Lax & Sebenius (1986), whose The Manager as Negotiator remains the definitive treatment.

The mechanics of claiming value

In distributive bargaining, the determinative quantities are the parties' reservation prices (walk-away points, anchored on each side's BATNA — Lesson 7) and the ZOPA they define. The dominant levers are: anchoring — first offers exert a powerful, well-documented pull on outcomes (Tversky & Kahneman, 1974; Galinsky & Mussweiler, 2001) because subsequent figures adjust insufficiently from the anchor; concession patterns — graduated, decreasing concessions signal approach to one's limit; and information control — concealing one's reservation price while estimating the counterpart's. These tactics are necessary but, used exclusively, leave integrative value unrealized.

The engines of value creation

Integrative agreement is generated by exploiting differences and shared interests (Lax & Sebenius, 1986; Fisher, Ury & Patton, 1981):

  • Differences in priorities (logrolling): each party concedes on low-priority issues to gain on high-priority ones. This is the workhorse of value creation and requires multiple issues.
  • Differences in forecasts: resolved via contingent contracts that pay out according to which party's prediction proves correct.
  • Differences in risk attitude: the risk-tolerant party bears more risk for a premium.
  • Differences in time preference: payment and delivery schedules are adjusted to each party's discount rate.
  • Shared interests and economies of scale: cooperation that grows total resources.
  • Adding issues / bundling: expanding the negotiation's scope to create tradeable currency.

The normative target is the Pareto frontier: the locus of agreements from which no reallocation can help one party without harming the other. Negotiators routinely settle inside the frontier, leaving "money on the table" — Pareto-inferior outcomes that better process could have improved.

The fixed-pie bias and the negotiator's dilemma

The chief cognitive obstacle is the mythical fixed pie (Bazerman & Neale, 1992): negotiators assume their interests are diametrically opposed even when they are compatible or differentially weighted, and so fail to discover trades. Experimental studies repeatedly find that negotiators with identical or compatible interests nonetheless reach impasse or suboptimal deals because they presume conflict. Compounding this is the negotiator's dilemma (Lax & Sebenius, 1986): the moves that create value (openness, information sharing, trust) are precisely those that expose one to exploitation by a value-claimer, while the moves that claim value (concealment, hard positions) suppress the information needed to create it. The dilemma has no clean solution; the practiced response is reciprocal, incremental disclosure of interests — revealing priorities (not reservation prices), proposing packages and multiple equivalent offers (MESOs) to elicit information, and conditioning further openness on the counterpart's reciprocation.

Worked example with calculation logic

A software vendor and a corporate buyer negotiate three issues: license price, contract length, and support level. Suppose the buyer's priorities (points) are price 60, length 10, support 30; the vendor's are price 30, length 50 (long contracts smooth revenue), support 20. A price-only (distributive) fight ignores that the vendor values length far more than the buyer does, and the buyer values price more than the vendor does. The logrolling trade: the buyer commits to a longer contract (cheap for the buyer — 10 points — but worth 50 to the vendor), and in exchange the vendor lowers price (worth 60 to the buyer, costing the vendor only 30). Both capture more total value than a split-the-difference price deal — they have moved toward the Pareto frontier by trading along their differing priorities. Adding a contingent clause (a discount that triggers if the buyer's usage exceeds a forecast) can capture a difference-in-forecasts gain on top.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Assuming the fixed pie — the single most expensive and most common error.
  • Treating distributive and integrative as mutually exclusive — virtually all real deals are mixed-motive; you create value and then must claim it.
  • Single-issue tunnel vision — without multiple issues there is nothing to logroll; sophisticated negotiators add issues to create trades.
  • Premature value claiming — anchoring hard before exploring interests can poison the information exchange and lock in a smaller pie.
  • Over-disclosure — revealing your reservation price (as opposed to your interests) hands away your claiming leverage.
  • Confusing compromise with integration — splitting the difference on one issue is distributive; true integration trades across issues so both gain more than a compromise yields.

Cross-links

This foundation organizes the remainder of the course. Lesson 6 (principled negotiation / Getting to Yes) is essentially a disciplined method for integrative bargaining — separating people from problem, focusing on interests, inventing options, using objective criteria. Lesson 7 (BATNA, reservation price, ZOPA) supplies the analytic machinery of value claiming and the discipline that protects you while you create value. Lesson 8 (tactics, biases, multiparty, ethics) examines the distributive tactics, cognitive biases (anchoring, fixed-pie), and ethical tensions that arise as parties navigate the create-versus-claim tension at the table. The communication skills of Lessons 1-4 are the means by which interests are surfaced and trades discovered.
